%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Frequency Response and Pole-Zero Plots

## Section Objective

> Connect discrete-time transfer functions to frequency response and pole-zero plots used in MATLAB.

## Concepts In This Section

- rational transfer function
- MATLAB coefficient vectors
- frequency response on the unit circle
- pole-zero plot
- extra roots at the origin

## 1. Rational transfer function

Eq. (5.54) represents a realizable discrete-time LTID system as a ratio of two polynomials in the \(z\)-domain.

The numerator coefficients \(b_k\) are feedforward coefficients. The denominator coefficients \(a_k\) are feedback coefficients. In this full form, \(N\) is the common maximum order used for both sums.

The meaning is direct: \(H[z]\) tells how the system transforms an input \(X[z]\) into an output \(Y[z]\). Start here when a problem gives a difference equation or coefficient list and asks for system behavior.

### COMMON MISTAKE

Do not treat numerator coefficients and denominator coefficients as interchangeable.

For example, \(B=[1,2]\) means \(b_0=1\), \(b_1=2\), not a denominator term.

$$H[z] = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{N} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}} \quad (5.54)$$

## 2. MATLAB-friendly coefficient form

Eq. (5.55) rewrites the transfer function so MATLAB can evaluate ordinary polynomials using coefficient vectors.

Here, \(N_1\) is the numerator order and \(N_2\) is the denominator order. The coefficient vectors are \(B=[b_0,b_1,\ldots,b_{N_1}]\) and \(A=[a_0,a_1,\ldots,a_{N_2}]\).

The extra factor \(z^{N_2-N_1}\) matters whenever the numerator and denominator orders differ. This form is the exam trigger when MATLAB code uses `polyval(B,z)` and `polyval(A,z)`.

### COMMON MISTAKE

Forgetting \(z^{N_2-N_1}\) gives the wrong roots when \(N_1\ne N_2\).

For example, if \(N_2-N_1=1\), the expression includes one extra factor of \(z\).

$$H[z] = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{N_1} b_k z^{N_1-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N_2} a_k z^{N_2-k}}\,z^{N_2-N_1} \quad (5.55)$$

## 3. Frequency response means evaluate on the unit circle

The frequency response is not a new transfer function. It is the same \(H[z]\), evaluated at points \(z=e^{j\Omega}\) on the unit circle.

Here, \(\Omega\) is discrete-time radian frequency, typically between \(-\pi\) and \(\pi\). Often, \(\Omega=\omega T\), where \(\omega\) is continuous-time radian frequency and \(T\) is the sampling period.

### EXAM TRIGGER

When asked for frequency response, substitute \(z=e^{j\Omega}\).

### COMMON MISTAKE

Do not substitute \(z=\Omega\), and do not treat \(\Omega\) as a radius.

At \(\Omega=0\), the evaluation point is \(z=e^{j0}=1\).

$$H[e^{j\Omega}] = H[z]\big|_{z=e^{j\Omega}}$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

## 4. How CH5MP1 evaluates the formula

The MATLAB code recovers polynomial orders from vector lengths: `N_1 = length(B)-1` and `N_2 = length(A)-1`.

Then `polyval(B,exp(1j*Omega))` evaluates the numerator polynomial at \(e^{j\Omega}\). Similarly, `polyval(A,exp(1j*Omega))` evaluates the denominator polynomial at the same unit-circle points.

### COMMON MISTAKE

MATLAB indexing starts at \(1\), but the textbook coefficients start at \(0\). Therefore, `B(k)` corresponds to \(b_{k-1}\), and `A(k)` corresponds to \(a_{k-1}\).

A near-miss is saying `B(1)` equals \(b_1\). That is wrong: `B(1)` equals \(b_0\).


## 5. Extra roots at the origin

Eq. (5.55) contains the factor \(z^{N_2-N_1}\). This factor determines whether unequal polynomial orders create extra roots at \(z=0\).

If \(N_2>N_1\), then \(\Delta>0\). The positive factor \(z^{N_2-N_1}\) stays in the numerator, so it contributes \(N_2-N_1\) extra zeros at the origin.

If \(N_1>N_2\), the exponent is negative. That moves \(z^{N_1-N_2}\) into the denominator, so it contributes \(N_1-N_2\) extra poles at the origin.

### EXAM TRIGGER

Apply this when MATLAB pads `B` or `A` with zeros before calling `roots`.

### COMMON MISTAKE

Do not add extra roots to the wrong side. If \(N_1=3\) and \(N_2=1\), there are two extra poles at \(z=0\), not two extra zeros.

$$\Delta = N_2 - N_1$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Eq. (5.54): \(H[z]=\frac{\sum_{k=0}^{N} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}\).
- Eq. (5.55): \(H[z]=\frac{\sum_{k=0}^{N_1} b_k z^{N_1-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N_2} a_k z^{N_2-k}}z^{N_2-N_1}\).
- Frequency response: \(H[e^{j\Omega}]=H[z]|_{z=e^{j\Omega}}\), so evaluate around the unit circle.
- MATLAB indexing trap: `B(1)` is \(b_0\), and `A(1)` is \(a_0\).
- Root-order rule: \(N_2>N_1\) gives extra zeros; \(N_1>N_2\) gives extra poles at \(z=0\).

*Next, we will use these ideas to transform and implement discrete-time IIR filters in MATLAB.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
